summary: In this podcast interview, Jeff Dean discusses Google's approach to AI model development, emphasizing the dual need for highly capable frontier models and efficient distilled models. He shares historical insights from scaling Google Search, principles for system design and hardware-software co-design, and explains the energy-based rationale behind batching and low-precision ML computations. In this interview, Jeff Dean discusses the evolution of AI hardware and software co-design, the shift from specialized to general unified models, and the importance of scaling and sparsity. He also reflects on the organizational consolidation that led to Google Gemini and shares his perspective on the future of AI-assisted software engineering. Jeff Dean discusses the importance of low-latency models, the value of multimodal prompting, and how providing coding agents with general software engineering guides can improve their output. He also predicts that highly personalized models and specialized hardware will drive the next wave of AI utility, enabling massive token generation for chain-of-thought reasoning.

principles.statement: Both frontier models and distilled models are necessary; they are not an either/or choice.
principles.rationale: Frontier models push the boundaries of what is possible and handle deep reasoning, while distillation uses those frontier capabilities to create smaller, affordable, low-latency models for broader use cases.
principles.source_quote: it's not that one or the other is useful. They're both useful... you have to have the frontier model in order to then distill it into your your smaller model. So it's not like an either or choice.

principles.statement: Design systems to scale by factors of 5 or 10, but not 100x.
principles.rationale: If a system needs to scale by 100x, that massive scale usually enables a completely different and more efficient point in the design space that wouldn't make sense at the original scale.
principles.source_quote: you're want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10 but probably not beyond that because often what happens is if you design a system for X and something suddenly becomes 100X that would enable a very different point in the design space

principles.statement: Multimodal AI should include non-human modalities.
principles.rationale: Exposing models to data like LiDAR, X-rays, or genomics, even in small amounts, teaches the model that these modalities exist and have meaning in the world.
principles.source_quote: it's also really useful to have Gemini know about nonhuman modalities. like LAR sensor data... at least including a little bit of it is actually quite useful because it sort of tempts the model that this is a thing.

principles.statement: Hardware and software must be co-designed based on where the ML research puck is going.
principles.rationale: Hardware takes years to design and deploy, so hardware teams need ML researchers to predict what computations will be important 2-6 years out, while ML researchers must adapt architectures to run efficiently on upcoming chips.
principles.source_quote: we really want to take advantage of being able to co-design what should future TPUs look like based on where we think the sort of ML research puck is going

principles.statement: General models will win out over specialized ones in most cases.
principles.rationale: Emulating the brain's distributed representation is more effective than building completely separate, discrete symbolic systems for different tasks.
principles.source_quote: general models will win out over specialized ones in most cases

principles.statement: Devote parameter space to reasoning, not memorization.
principles.rationale: It is inefficient to use precious model capacity to memorize obscure facts that can easily be retrieved. Models should focus on reasoning and use retrieval as a tool.
principles.source_quote: having the model devote precious parameter space to remember obscure facts that could be looked up is actually not the best use of that parameter space

principles.statement: Build sparsely activated models to balance capacity and efficiency.
principles.rationale: You want models to have massive capacity to remember things, but you need them to be highly efficient during inference by only activating a small percentage of the network.
principles.source_quote: you'd like you know trillions of parameters but activate only you know one 1% or 5% or 10% of that

principles.statement: Crisply specify what you want instead of leaving things to ambiguity.
principles.rationale: As AI coding agents become more capable, the quality of their output is directly dictated by how carefully and clearly the human specifies the requirements and corner cases.
principles.source_quote: the better you get at interacting with these models and and I think one of the ways people will get better is they will get really good at crisply specifying things rather than leaving things to ambiguity

principles.statement: Pushing on latency is critical for user experience and system capabilities.
principles.rationale: Low-latency interactions are much more delightful for users and allow underlying systems to perform complex background tasks (like parallel rollouts) between interactions.
principles.source_quote: I'm a big believer in pushing on latency because I think being able to have really low latency interactions with a system you're using is just much more delightful than something that is, you know, 10 times as slow

principles.statement: Personalized models will vastly outperform generic models in utility.
principles.rationale: A model that can securely retrieve and attend to a user's entire personal state (emails, photos, videos) provides significantly more contextual value than a generic model.
principles.source_quote: a personalized model that knows you and knows all your state and is able to retrieve over all state you have access to that you opt into is going to be incredibly useful compared to a more generic model

principles.statement: High token throughput should be spent on reasoning, not just output.
principles.rationale: Generating thousands of tokens per second allows models to perform extensive chain-of-thought reasoning and verification before outputting a smaller, highly accurate final result (like code).
principles.source_quote: it may not end up with 10,000 tokens of code a thousand tokens of code that with 9,000 tokens of reasoning behind it. Which would actually be probably much better code to read.

frameworks.name: System Design Scaling Assessment
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing a new system or evaluating its capacity to handle future growth.
steps: Identify the most important design parameters (e.g., queries per second, index size, data per document).
Evaluate how the system will perform if traffic doubles or triples.
Design the system to comfortably scale by a factor of 5 or 10.
Avoid designing for 100x scale; instead, recognize that 100x scale will require and enable a completely new architecture.
frameworks.source_quote: whenever you're designing a system you want to understand what are the sort of design parameters that are going to be most important... a good design principle is you're want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10 but probably not beyond that

mental_models.name: Energy-Based Analysis of Computation
mental_models.description: Viewing the cost of ML operations not just in time, but in the energy (picojoules) required to move data. Moving data across a chip or from memory is orders of magnitude more expensive than the math operation itself.
mental_models.example: Explaining why batching is necessary: moving a parameter from SRAM to the multiplier costs 1000 picojoules, while the multiply costs 1 picojoule. Batching reuses that moved parameter across multiple inputs to amortize the massive energy cost of data movement.

mental_models.name: Attending to the Internet
mental_models.description: The ultimate goal of long-context models is giving the model the illusion of attending to trillions of tokens (the whole internet, all personal state) via a funnel of retrieval and ranking, similar to how Google Search filters the web.
mental_models.example: Using highly parallel, lightweight models to filter trillions of tokens down to 30,000 candidates, then using a slightly more sophisticated model to narrow it to 117 documents, which are finally processed by the most capable frontier model.

mental_models.name: The Energy, Latency, and Throughput Lens
mental_models.description: Evaluating algorithmic improvements based on their physical hardware costs—real energy (joules per bit), latency, and throughput—rather than just theoretical metrics.
mental_models.example: Using this lens to evaluate speculative decoding, noting that it provides a 5x improvement in the amortization of moving weights into multipliers.

mental_models.name: AI Agents as a Team of Interns
mental_models.description: Reasoning about the future of AI-assisted software engineering by comparing AI agents to human interns, which requires hierarchical management and specific interaction models.
mental_models.example: Instead of interacting with 50 individual agents directly, a developer might manage 5 sub-teams of agents, similar to managing a 50-person human organization.

mental_models.name: Prompting as Executive Communication
mental_models.description: Treating prompt engineering like writing an internal memo to an executive, where words must be weighed carefully and intent must be clearly specified.
mental_models.example: When writing a prompt, you weigh your words very carefully, just as you would when communicating with a high-level executive.

heuristics: Design systems to scale by factors of 5 or 10, but probably not beyond that.
Think through designs in your head and play with the design space before writing a lot of code.
Benchmarks are most useful when initial scores are 10-30%; once they hit 95%, you get diminishing returns.
Use very low precision with scaling vectors to save energy on data transfer.
Bigger model, more data, better results.
Reduce bit precision to save energy, because it's joules per bit transferred.
Use video as the highest bandwidth communication prompt for multimodal models.
Provide coding agents with general software engineering guides (e.g., 20 distributed systems techniques) rather than writing custom prompts for every edge case.

signature_quotes.text: I think what you would really want is can I attend to the internet while I answer my question right
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the future of long-context windows in LLMs.

signature_quotes.text: You're want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10 but probably not beyond that because often what happens is if you design a system for X and something suddenly becomes 100X that would enable a very different point in the design space
signature_quotes.context: Explaining system design principles and the history of scaling Google Search.

signature_quotes.text: Ideally you'd like to use batch size one because the latency would be great but the energy cost and the compute cost inefficiency that you get is quite large.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the energy economics of data movement in AI accelerators.

signature_quotes.text: Once you have the whole index in memory, it's totally fine to have 50 terms you throw into the query... you can suddenly start sort of really getting at the meaning of the word as opposed to the exact semantic form.
signature_quotes.context: Recalling the 2001 shift to an in-memory index for Google Search.

signature_quotes.text: I'm a big fan of very low precision because I think that gets that saves you a tremendous amount of energy, right? Because it's joules per bit that you're transferring.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing hardware and software co-design for model efficiency.

signature_quotes.text: General models will win out over specialized ones in most cases.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the shift from specialized models (like AlphaGeometry) to unified models (like Gemini).

signature_quotes.text: Having the model devote precious parameter space to remember obscure facts that could be looked up is actually not the best use of that parameter space.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the tension between model capacity, memorization, and retrieval.

signature_quotes.text: Bigger model, more data, better results.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the mantra that drove Google's neural network scaling efforts for six or seven years.

signature_quotes.text: good prompting is indistinguishable from sufficiently advanced executive communication.
signature_quotes.context: Comparing prompt engineering to writing an internal memo.

signature_quotes.text: I'm a big believer in pushing on latency because I think being able to have really low latency interactions with a system you're using is just much more delightful
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the importance of fast AI models.

signature_quotes.text: it may not end up with 10,000 tokens of code a thousand tokens of code that with 9,000 tokens of reasoning behind it. Which would actually be probably much better code to read.
signature_quotes.context: On the future of high token-per-second generation and chain of thought.

anti_patterns.description: Designing a system to scale 100x from the start.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: A 100x scale usually enables a completely different, more efficient point in the design space that wouldn't make sense at the current scale (e.g., moving from disk-based to in-memory indexes).
Once a benchmark hits ~95%, focusing on it yields diminishing returns and risks leakage or overfitting, rather than building genuinely new capabilities.

anti_patterns.description: Fragmenting organizational resources across multiple competing AI efforts.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It divides compute quotas, the best people, and the best ideas, preventing the creation of a single, unified, state-of-the-art model.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on analog computing substrates for AI hardware.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The theoretical power advantages are often lost during the analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog conversions required at the system's periphery.

anti_patterns.description: Building completely separate discrete symbolic systems alongside neural nets.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It doesn't emulate how real brains intuitively work, which rely on distributed representations rather than discrete symbolic manipulation.

anti_patterns.description: Spending a long time writing a massive, highly-specified prompt for a slow, smart model instead of iterating quickly with a fast model.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Because user intent is often underspecified, a single large prompt might still produce the wrong output, whereas fast, multi-turn iteration allows for quick human alignment and correction.
